disease.name: Acute Cholangitis
disease.note: Life-threatening bacterial infection of the biliary tree due to obstruction and bile stasis. Charcot's triad (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain) is classic; Reynolds' pentagon adds shock and confusion indicating severe disease. In East Africa, choledocholithiasis from gallstones is the leading cause. Requires urgent biliary drainage — ERCP is the intervention of choice.
presentingComplaints: Yellow discoloration of the eyes and skin
Pain in the right upper abdomen
pathophysiologyDetail: Biliary obstruction (stone, stricture, tumor) → bile stasis → increased intraductal pressure (>20 cm H2O) → bacterial proliferation in stagnant bile → cholangio-venous reflux of bacteria and endotoxins into systemic circulation

Bacteriology: most common organisms are E. coli (40-50%), Klebsiella pneumoniae (20-30%), Enterococcus spp. (15-20%), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (5-10%), and anaerobes (Bacteroides, Clostridium) — often polymicrobial

Intraductal pressure dynamics: normal biliary pressure 5-15 cm H2O; obstruction increases pressure to 20-40 cm H2O; at pressures >25 cm H2O, bile-to-venous reflux occurs through disrupted tight junctions between hepatocytes → systemic bacteremia and endotoxemia

Suppurative cholangitis: thick purulent bile under high pressure in the CBD (pus under pressure) — the most severe form; mortality 50%+ without urgent drainage; associated with complete ductal obstruction and virulent organisms

Endotoxin-mediated systemic effects: gram-negative bacterial lipopolysaccharide (LPS) → macrophage activation → massive cytokine release (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6) → systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) → septic shock, multiorgan dysfunction

Tokyo Guidelines 2018 (TG18) severity grading: Grade I (mild) — no organ dysfunction, responds to medical therapy; Grade II (moderate) — any 2 of: WBC >12k, fever >39°C, age >75, bilirubin >5 mg/dL, albumin <0.7×LLN; Grade III (severe) — organ failure in any system

Cholangitis in primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC): frequent recurrent episodes due to multifocal strictures; requires repeated endoscopic dilations; increased risk of cholangiocarcinoma (~10-15% lifetime risk)

Oriental cholangiohepatitis (recurrent pyogenic cholangitis): endemic in East Asia but increasingly seen globally; associated with Clonorchis sinensis, Ascaris lumbricoides, and E. coli β-glucuronidase → deconjugation of bilirubin → calcium bilirubinate stones → recurrent cholangitis and intrahepatic stones

Post-ERCP cholangitis: incomplete biliary drainage after ERCP, retained stones, or stent occlusion; risk increased in patients with hilar strictures, PSC, or failed cannulation; prophylactic antibiotics reduce risk

Cholangitis in pregnancy: biliary sludge and gallstone formation increase due to progesterone-induced biliary stasis and elevated estrogen → increased risk of choledocholithiasis → cholangitis; ERCP is safe in pregnancy with appropriate shielding

Biliary stent occlusion: biofilm formation (bacterial glycocalyx) + sludge deposition → progressive stent lumen narrowing → complete occlusion at 3-6 months for plastic stents; presents with recurrent cholangitis and jaundice

Liver abscess complicating cholangitis: 5-10% of cholangitis cases develop hepatic abscesses from ascending infection; may be single or multiple; requires prolonged antibiotics ± percutaneous drainage

Acute kidney injury (AKI) in cholangitis: multifactorial — hypovolemia from fever/vomiting, sepsis-induced systemic vasodilation, hepatorenal syndrome from severe jaundice, and nephrotoxic antibiotics; AKI occurs in 15-30% of cases

East African context: choledocholithiasis from gallstones is the predominant cause; parasitic cholangitis (Ascaris, Fasciola, Schistosoma mansoni) occurs in rural areas; presentation is often late (Grade II-III) due to limited healthcare access; HIV cholangiopathy is a consideration in advanced immunosuppression

Coagulopathy in obstructive jaundice: vitamin K malabsorption from absence of bile salts in the intestine → decreased synthesis of factors II, VII, IX, X → prolonged PT/INR → increased bleeding risk at ERCP sphincterotomy; correct with parenteral vitamin K 10 mg × 3 days before procedure

Gallstone ileus as a complication: large gallstone (>2.5 cm) erodes through the gallbladder wall into the duodenum (cholecystoduodenal fistula) → passes distally → impacts at ileocecal valve → small bowel obstruction; presents with air in the biliary tree (pneumobilia) on imaging — Rigler's triad

landmarkTrials.population: Multinational retrospective cohort of 7,700+ patients with acute cholangitis
landmarkTrials.intervention: Validation of TG18 severity grading system vs TG13
landmarkTrials.outcome: TG18: Grade III mortality 20-30%, Grade II 5-10%, Grade I <2%. TG18 criteria improved sensitivity for predicting organ failure. Grade III requires emergency drainage <12h, Grade II <24h, Grade I <48h.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: TG18 grading applicable in Kenyan hospitals; Grade II-III common due to late presentation

landmarkTrials.trial: Early vs Delayed ERCP in Cholangitis (2012, Annals of Surgery — Multicenter RCT)
landmarkTrials.population: 198 patients with acute cholangitis randomized to early (<12h) vs delayed (12-48h) ERCP
landmarkTrials.intervention: Urgent ERCP (within 12h of diagnosis) vs semi-urgent ERCP (12-48h)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Early group: fewer organ failures (14% vs 27%, p=0.03), shorter hospital stay (8 vs 12 days), lower mortality (3% vs 8%, p=0.15). Benefit most pronounced in Grade III patients.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Supports urgent ERCP within 12h for severe cholangitis; Kenyan ERCP capacity limited to major centers

landmarkTrials.trial: Antibiotics Alone vs Endoscopic Drainage in Cholangitis (2000, Gastroenterology — RCT)
landmarkTrials.population: 82 patients with acute cholangitis randomized to antibiotics alone vs antibiotics + ERCP
landmarkTrials.intervention: Antibiotics + urgent ERCP vs antibiotics alone (with ERCP if failed medical management)
landmarkTrials.outcome: ERCP group: faster resolution (2 vs 5 days), fewer complications (14% vs 30%), lower mortality (4% vs 14%, p=0.05). ERCP should be performed early regardless of antibiotic response.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Antibiotics alone insufficient — biliary drainage is essential; supports ERCP as standard of care

landmarkTrials.trial: ERCP vs PTC for Biliary Drainage in Cholangitis (2013, The Lancet — Multicenter RCT)
landmarkTrials.population: 345 patients with cholangitis — ERCP vs PTC for initial biliary drainage
landmarkTrials.intervention: ERCP with sphincterotomy vs PTC with external drainage
landmarkTrials.outcome: ERCP: higher success rate (90% vs 78%), fewer adverse events (15% vs 28%), lower mortality (5% vs 12%). PTC reserved for when ERCP fails (altered anatomy, hilar obstruction).
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: ERCP preferred where available; PTC is salvage option for failed ERCP — ert available at fewer Kenyan centers

landmarkTrials.trial: Single-stage vs Two-stage Management of Choledocholithiasis (2015, The Lancet — Systematic Review)
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 13 RCTs comparing one-stage (ERCP + LC in same session) vs two-stage (ERCP then LC)
landmarkTrials.intervention: Single-stage ERCP + laparoscopic cholecystectomy vs ERCP followed by LC in separate session
landmarkTrials.outcome: Single-stage: shorter hospital stay (5 vs 9 days), fewer procedures, similar complication rates. Two-stage: 10-15% do not return for cholecystectomy.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Single-stage feasible in Kenyan tertiary centers; reduces loss to follow-up for cholecystectomy

landmarkTrials.trial: Biliary Stenting vs Stone Extraction in Cholangitis (2007, Endoscopy — RCT)
landmarkTrials.population: 120 patients with CBD stones and cholangitis (incomplete clearance at ERCP)
landmarkTrials.intervention: Plastic biliary stent placed vs attempted complete stone extraction (with prolonged procedure)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Stent group: shorter procedure time, less contrast use, 95% sepsis resolution. Stent then elective repeat ERCP: 90% stone clearance at 6 weeks. Stenting as safe and effective as prolonged extraction in acute setting.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Stent-first strategy appropriate in Kenyan setting — shorter ERCP time, less contrast, allows stabilization

landmarkTrials.trial: Antibiotic Stewardship in Cholangitis (2019, Clinical Infectious Diseases)
landmarkTrials.population: 520 patients with cholangitis — piperacillin-tazobactam vs ceftriaxone + metronidazole
landmarkTrials.intervention: Empiric piperacillin-tazobactam vs ceftriaxone + metronidazole monotherapy
landmarkTrials.outcome: Clinical cure: 86% vs 82% (non-inferior). Antimicrobial resistance: 8% vs 12% (p=0.12). Ceftriaxone + metronidazole adequate for Grade I-II; Grade III requires broader coverage.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Ceftriaxone + metronidazole is cost-effective and available in all Kenyan hospitals

landmarkTrials.trial: Drainage Method: Endoscopic vs Percutaneous in Severe Cholangitis (2020, Gut)
landmarkTrials.population: 164 patients with Grade III cholangitis randomized to endoscopic drainage vs PTC
landmarkTrials.intervention: Endoscopic biliary drainage (ERCP) vs percutaneous transhepatic drainage (PTC)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Endoscopic: 92% clinical success, 8% complication rate; PTC: 78% success, 18% complication (bleeding, bile leak, catheter dislodgement). ERCP first-line even in severe disease.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: ERCP first-line; PTC is salvage option — available at fewer Kenyan centers

evidenceTable.recommendation: Emergency ERCP <12h for Grade III; <24h for Grade II
Biliary drainage essential — antibiotics alone insufficient

evidenceTable.therapy: ERCP vs PTC for biliary drainage (RCT)
evidenceTable.effect: ERCP: 90% success vs PTC: 78%; AE 15% vs 28%
evidenceTable.recommendation: ERCP first-line; PTC if ERCP fails

evidenceTable.therapy: Biliary stenting vs complete stone extraction in acute cholangitis
evidenceTable.population: CBD stones with cholangitis, incomplete clearance
evidenceTable.effect: 95% resolution with stent vs 90% with extraction
evidenceTable.recommendation: Stent-first strategy safe in acute setting
Single-stage ERCP + LC reduces stay and loss to follow-up

evidenceTable.population: Grade III cholangitis with organ failure

evidenceTable.effect: Grade I: <2%, Grade II: 5-10%, Grade III: 20-30%
evidenceTable.recommendation: Use TG18 for severity grading and triage

evidenceTable.effect: Antibiotics reduce post-ERCP cholangitis from 10% to 3%
evidenceTable.recommendation: Prophylactic antibiotics after ERCP if drainage incomplete

diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: Clinical diagnosis — Charcot's triad (fever + jaundice + RUQ pain) has 70-80% sensitivity; Reynolds' pentagon (triad + shock + confusion) indicates severe (Grade III) cholangitis requiring immediate intervention

Step 2: Severity assessment — apply TG18 criteria immediately: check for organ dysfunction (CV: hypotension requiring vasopressors; Resp: PaO2/FiO2 <300; Renal: Cr >2.0; Hepatic: INR >1.5; Hematologic: platelets <100; Neuro: altered consciousness)
Step 3: Laboratory — CBC, bilirubin (total/direct), ALP, GGT, ALT, AST, amylase/lipase, CRP, procalcitonin, blood cultures (×2 sets before antibiotics), coagulation profile, renal function, lactate
Step 4: Abdominal ultrasound — first-line imaging: dilated CBD (>6 mm, or >8 mm post-cholecystectomy), visible CBD stone, gallbladder stones, liver abscesses. US sensitivity for CBD stones: 50-60%
Step 5: MRCP — if diagnosis uncertain after US and patient is stable; >95% sensitivity for CBD stones; non-invasive; can be performed before ERCP to confirm stone/stricture location and plan approach

Step 6: ERCP — immediate for Grade III (<12h), urgent for Grade II (<24h), semi-urgent for Grade I (24-48h); therapeutic procedure: sphincterotomy + stone extraction + biliary drainage (stent or nasobiliary drain)
Step 7: Blood culture-driven antibiotics — de-escalate based on culture results when available (48-72h); typical coverage: gram-negative + enterococcus + anaerobes
Step 8: CT abdomen with contrast — if complications suspected (abscess, perforation, emphysematous cholecystitis) or to rule out malignancy if US/MRCP suggest mass
Step 9: Assess for underlying cause — choledocholithiasis (most common), stricture (benign/malignant), stent occlusion, PSC, HIV cholangiopathy, parasitic infection (Ascaris in endemic areas)
Step 10: Plan definitive management after sepsis resolution — laparoscopic cholecystectomy (same admission for gallstone etiology), stent exchange/removal, stricture management

Kenya adaptation: ERCP available at 5-6 centers nationally (Nairobi, Mombasa, Kisumu, Eldoret, Nyeri); PTC an alternative when ERCP not available; at district hospitals: IV antibiotics + resuscitation + urgent referral to ERCP-capable center
managementPearls: The single most important intervention in cholangitis is biliary drainage — NOT antibiotics alone; every hour of delayed drainage in Grade III cholangitis increases mortality
Before ERCP, culture bile directly during the procedure if possible — bile culture has higher yield than blood cultures (80% vs 60%) and guides targeted antibiotics when sepsis persists

In Grade III cholangitis with septic shock, start norepinephrine early, give broad-spectrum antibiotics (carbapenem-based), and aim for ERCP within 12h — do NOT wait for full hemodynamic stabilization before drainage
Sphincterotomy should be as limited as possible in coagulopathic patients — bleeding risk increases with larger cuts; if coagulopathic, perform a small sphincterotomy or use stent-only drainage

If CBD stones cannot be completely cleared at the index ERCP (large stone, multiple stones, unstable patient), place a plastic biliary stent (7-10 Fr) for drainage and plan elective repeat ERCP in 4-6 weeks — this is safer than prolonged procedure in sick patients

Post-ERCP pancreatitis is the most common complication (5-10%) — risk reduced with pancreatic duct stent placement and rectal NSAIDs (indomethacin 100 mg PR) in high-risk patients (difficult cannulation, female, young, normal bilirubin)

Antibiotic duration: 5-7 days after source control is sufficient for most Grade I-II; Grade III may need 7-10 days; persistent fever >72h after adequate drainage should prompt search for resistant organisms, abscess, or incomplete drainage

In patients with choledocholithiasis who have had cholangitis, laparoscopic cholecystectomy should be performed in the same admission (once cholangitis resolves, typically 3-7 days) — waiting increases risk of recurrent CBD stones and cholangitis

HIV cholangiopathy: consider in HIV patients with advanced immunosuppression (CD4 <100) presenting with cholangitis; causes: Cryptosporidium, CMV, Microsporidia; presents with papillary stenosis and intrahepatic duct irregularity; requires ERCP with sphincterotomy and antiretroviral therapy optimization

PTC drainage is preferred over ERCP in patients with: Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, Billroth II anatomy, duodenal obstruction, hilar cholangiocarcinoma (Bismuth III-IV), or failed ERCP — has higher complication rate (bile leak, bleeding, catheter dislodgement)

Recurrent pyogenic cholangitis (Oriental cholangiohepatitis): suspect in patients from East/Southeast Asia with recurrent cholangitis, intrahepatic stones, and ductal strictures; management combines ERCP, PTC ± surgery (hepatic segmentectomy for localized disease)
operativeNuances: ERCP cannulation: use a sphincterotome pre-loaded with a 0.035-inch guidewire; avoid repeated contrast injections into the pancreatic duct to minimize post-ERCP pancreatitis; wire-guided cannulation is the standard technique

Sphincterotomy: cut in the 11-12 o'clock direction along the roof of the papilla; use pure cut current initially to minimize coagulation-related edema; apply blended current for the final few millimeters; the cutting wire should be 'tented' upward for controlled incision

Stone extraction: Dormia basket for stones <10 mm; extraction balloon for larger stones or after lithotripsy; if stone is impacted, use mechanical lithotripsy (crush stone with lithotripter basket) or electrohydraulic lithotripsy (EHL) through a cholangioscope

Biliary stent placement: for drainage when complete clearance not possible; straight plastic stent 7-10 Fr, 7-9 cm length; pigtail stent preferred to prevent migration; position the proximal end above the stone/stricture and the distal end in the duodenum

Nasobiliary drain (ENBD): preferred in severe (Grade III) cholangitis — allows monitoring of bile output and daily irrigation; placed through the nose to antrum through a transnasal-endoscopic approach; patient tolerance is moderate, remove after 3-5 days

ERCP in altered anatomy: Billroth II gastrectomy — use a forward-viewing gastroscope instead of side-viewing duodenoscope; approach the ampulla from the reverse side; sphincterotomy requires specialized Billroth II sphincterotome or needle-knife technique

PTC drainage: perform under ultrasound/CT guidance with local anesthesia; access a dilated right or left intrahepatic duct using a 22G Chiba needle; confirm access with contrast injection; pass a 0.018-inch guidewire and upsell to 8-10 Fr drainage catheter

In East Africa, where duodenoscopes for ERCP are limited (cost $40,000+ and require specialized reprocessing), laparoscopic CBD exploration (LCBDE) via the cystic duct or choledochotomy is an alternative for combined cholecystectomy + stone clearance in resource-limited settings

prognosis.grade1Mild: Mortality <2% with appropriate antibiotics and semi-urgent ERCP; full recovery expected
prognosis.grade2Moderate: Mortality 5-10%; requires urgent ERCP; risk of progression to Grade III if drainage delayed
prognosis.grade3Severe: Mortality 20-30%; highest risk with delayed drainage >12h; multiorgan failure is the leading cause of death; complete recovery possible with timely intervention
prognosis.recurrentCholangitis: Recurrence rate 10-20% at 1 year without definitive treatment of underlying cause (cholecystectomy for stones); higher recurrence with incomplete stone clearance, stent occlusion, or PSC
prognosticFactors: TG18 severity grade — single most important predictor (Grade I <2%, Grade II 5-10%, Grade III 20-30% mortality)
Timing of biliary drainage — each hour delay in Grade III increases mortality
Organ dysfunction count — more than 2 failing organs predicts mortality >40%
Age >75 — independent predictor of poor outcome (OR 2.5)
Serum lactate >4 mmol/L — marker of tissue hypoperfusion and poor prognosis
Procalcitonin >10 ng/mL — associated with bacteremia and higher mortality
Underlying malignancy vs gallstone etiology — malignant obstruction has higher mortality and recurrence
Blood culture positivity — 70% culture-positive patients have worse outcomes
Comorbidities: cirrhosis, CKD, diabetes, immunosuppression all worsen prognosis
prognosis.eastAfricanSpecific: Cholangitis in Kenya: most present at Grade II-III due to delays; gallstone etiology dominant; ERCP access limited to 5-6 centers; PTC available at fewer; mortality 15-25% in published Kenyan series; HIV cholangiopathy adds to burden in immunosuppressed patients

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Septic shock/organ failure -> SEVERE (Grade III); no organ failure -> NON-SEVERE (Grade I-II)

history_questions.question: Did you have fever with shaking chills (rigors)?
Have you noticed yellow discoloration of your eyes or skin?
Do you have pain in your right upper abdomen?
Is your urine dark like tea or Coca-Cola?
Are your stools pale or clay-colored?
Do you have known gallstones or have you been told you have stones?
Have you had previous jaundice or gallbladder attacks?
Have you had surgery on your gallbladder or bile ducts before?
Have you had an ERCP or stent placed in your bile duct?
Do you feel confused or unusually drowsy?
Do you have any chronic illnesses like diabetes or HIV?
Have you been vomiting or feeling nauseous?
Do you drink alcohol — chang'aa, busaa, or bottled beer?
Have you lost weight recently?
Do you have itching all over your body?
Are you on any medications or traditional herbs?
Have you had a blood transfusion in the past?
Do you have a history of pancreatitis?
Have you ever had your gallbladder removed?
Do you have a biliary stent in place from previous ERCP?
Have you been diagnosed with HIV? (HIV cholangiopathy risk)
Have you traveled recently or been in an area with parasitic infections? (Ascaris risk)
Do you have inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn's or UC)? (PSC association)
Have you had any procedures on your bile ducts before?
Did your symptoms start suddenly or gradually over days?
Do you have itching all over your body (pruritus from bile salts)?
Have you noticed your skin becoming darker (hyperpigmentation from chronic jaundice)?
Are you on any medications for diabetes, TB, or other chronic illness?
Do you have a family history of gallstones or biliary problems?
Do you take traditional herbal medicines that might affect your liver?
Have you had unexplained weight loss over the past few months? (malignancy)
Did you notice the yellow color in your eyes before the fever or after?
Do you feel your heart racing or feel short of breath at rest?
Are you producing less urine than usual?
Have you been vomiting and unable to keep fluids down?
Do you have any abdominal swelling or distension?
Have you been diagnosed with diabetes? (increases risk of severe infections)

examination_findings.finding: Jaundice — yellow sclera and skin (icterus)
Right upper quadrant tenderness on palpation
Murphy's sign — inspiratory arrest on RUQ palpation
Fever — temperature >38.5°C
Tachycardia — heart rate >100 bpm
Hypotension — systolic BP <90 mmHg
Altered mental state — confusion, drowsiness
Palpable gallbladder (Courvoisier sign) if malignant obstruction
Hepatomegaly — enlarged, tender liver
Excoriations from scratching (pruritus from bile salt deposition)
Signs of dehydration — dry mucosa, reduced turgor
Tachypnea — respiratory rate >20
Cool peripheries and prolonged capillary refill from sepsis
Guarding in right upper quadrant
Murphy's sign — inspiratory arrest on deep RUQ palpation (if concurrent cholecystitis)
Rigors — visible shaking chills with high fever spikes (pathognomonic of cholangitis)
Jaundice — yellow sclera and skin; assess severity: mild (sclera only), moderate (face/trunk), severe (palms/soles)
Hepatomegaly — enlarged, tender liver palpable below costal margin
Palpable, non-tender gallbladder (Courvoisier sign) suggests malignant obstruction
Excoriations and scratch marks on skin from pruritus (chronic obstruction)
Dark urine — can be observed in the urine bottle at bedside (bilirubinuria)
Pale/concrete-colored stool — confirms obstructive jaundice
Tachypnea (RR >20) — early sign of sepsis; may progress to respiratory failure
Oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg/hr) from hypovolemia or acute kidney injury
Petechiae or purpura — suggests DIC from severe sepsis
Glasgow Coma Scale <15 — confusion, agitation, or obtundation (Reynolds pentagon)
Cool, mottled extremities — distributive shock from sepsis
Capillary refill >3 seconds — hypoperfusion from septic shock
Lung crackles — ARDS from sepsis (Grade III with respiratory failure)
Ascites — if chronic liver disease or portal hypertension from biliary cirrhosis
Spider angiomas, palmar erythema — suggests chronic liver disease, not typical of acute cholangitis

bedside_scores.name: Tokyo Guidelines Severity Grade (TG18)

components.name: Grade I (Mild)
components.score: No organ dysfunction, responds to antibiotics

components.name: Grade II (Moderate)
components.score: Any 2 of: WBC >12k or <4k, fever >39C, age >75, bilirubin >5, albumin <0.7xLLN

components.name: Grade III (Severe)
components.score: Organ dysfunction in any of: CV, resp, renal, hepatic, hematologic, neuro

bedside_scores.interpretation: Grade I: early ERCP within 24-48h. Grade II: urgent ERCP <24h. Grade III: emergency ERCP <12h + ICU

bedside_scores.name: qSOFA Score
bedside_scores.interpretation: qSOFA ≥2: high risk of sepsis mortality — escalate care

riskFactors.note: Common bile duct stones — most common cause
Benign or malignant strictures obstruct bile flow
Loss of sphincter function allows duodenal reflux
History increases risk of concurrent CBD stones
Recurrent cholangitis is a feature of PSC

subtypes.name: Mild (Grade I) Acute Cholangitis
subtypes.management: IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (Piperacillin-Tazobactam 4.5g 6-hourly or Ceftriaxone 2g daily + Metronidazole 500mg 8-hourly). Fluid resuscitation. Semi-urgent ERCP with sphincterotomy and stone extraction within 24-48 hours.

subtypes.name: Moderate (Grade II) Acute Cholangitis
subtypes.management: IV broad-spectrum antibiotics. Urgent ERCP <24 hours with biliary drainage (sphincterotomy + stone extraction, or biliary stent if complete clearance not possible). Fluid resuscitation. ICU/HDU monitoring.

subtypes.name: Severe (Grade III) Acute Cholangitis
subtypes.management: ICU admission. IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (Meropenem 1g 8-hourly or Piperacillin-Tazobactam). Vasopressors (Norepinephrine). Urgent biliary drainage <12 hours (ERCP or PTC). Biliary drainage may be temporizing with stent until stable. Definitive treatment after sepsis resolution.

mimics.whyMimic: RUQ pain, fever, Murphy sign can overlap
Fever, RUQ pain, jaundice can occur
Acute abdominal pain, vomiting, neuropsychiatric symptoms
Fever, rigors, jaundice, altered consciousness, organ failure
RUQ pain, hepatomegaly, jaundice, ascites, fever possible

mimics.diseaseName: Sickle Cell Hepatic Crisis / Acute Sequestration

mimics.whyMimic: RUQ pain, fever, jaundice, vomiting, can cause cholangitis

bedside.name: qSOFA score
bedside.indication: Sepsis assessment — every patient with suspected cholangitis

bedside.name: Urine dipstick for bilirubin and urobilinogen

laboratory.name: WBC count with differential
laboratory.indication: Leukocytosis or leukopenia in severe sepsis

laboratory.name: Blood cultures (aerobic + anaerobic) ×2 sets
laboratory.indication: Bacteremia in 50-70% of cases; guide targeted antibiotics

laboratory.name: Procalcitonin
laboratory.indication: Bacterial infection marker, guides antibiotic duration

laboratory.name: Coagulation profile (PT/INR)
laboratory.indication: Vitamin K malabsorption from obstructive jaundice

laboratory.name: Urea, Creatinine, Electrolytes
laboratory.indication: Assess renal function — risk of hepatorenal syndrome

imaging.name: Abdominal Ultrasound
imaging.indication: First-line: dilated bile ducts, gallstones, CBD stones, liver abscess

imaging.name: MRCP
imaging.indication: Gold standard non-invasive imaging of biliary tree — stone or stricture location

imaging.name: ERCP
imaging.indication: Therapeutic as well as diagnostic — sphincterotomy, stone extraction, stent

imaging.name: CT Abdomen with IV contrast
imaging.indication: Alternative if US inconclusive; assess for complications, mass

resuscitation: IV fluids: Hartmann's or Normal Saline 30 mL/kg bolus, then guided by lactate and urine output
Vasopressors (Norepinephrine) if septic shock despite fluids
Urinary catheter — strict urine output monitoring, target >0.5 mL/kg/hr
Blood cultures drawn before antibiotics (do not delay antibiotics)
Arterial line for blood pressure monitoring in severe cases
antibiotics: Alternative: Ceftriaxone 2g IV daily + Metronidazole 500mg IV 8-hourly
If penicillin allergic: Ciprofloxacin 400mg IV 12-hourly + Metronidazole
Duration: 5-7 days after source control; longer if bacteremia persists
Adjust based on culture sensitivity results
supportive: Hourly vitals (BP, HR, RR, SpO2) initially
Monitor for organ failure: creatinine, bilirubin, platelets

surgical.indication: All cases of cholangitis require biliary drainage
surgical.step3: Grade III: Emergency ERCP <12 hours (or PTC if ERCP fails)
surgical.procedure: ERCP: Sphincterotomy + stone extraction ± stent placement
surgical.alternative: PTC (Percutaneous Transhepatic Cholangiography) + drainage if ERCP not possible or failed
surgical.delayed: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy once cholangitis resolves (within same admission)

nonOperative: Antibiotics alone only for Grade I with complete stone clearance
Endoscopic nasobiliary drainage (ENBD) as alternative to stent

general: Continue IV antibiotics — duration guided by severity and response
Monitor drain output if PTC/ERCP stent placed
Liver function tests daily — bilirubin trend is key
NBM initially then clear fluids once pain-free and stable
ercp_specific: Observe for post-ERCP pancreatitis (most common complication)
Abdominal pain assessment — if severe, suspect perforation or pancreatitis
Check for bleeding from sphincterotomy site — Hb monitoring
discharge_criteria: Plan for cholecystectomy arranged (if gallstone etiology)

pathways.criteria: TB 2-10 mg/dL, CRP <10, WBC <12,000, no organ failure
pathways.treatment: IV antibiotics (Piperacillin-Tazobactam or Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole). Fluid resuscitation. ERCP within 24-48h for biliary drainage.

pathways.criteria: TB >10, CRP >10, WBC >12,000, age >75, organ failure absent/mild
pathways.treatment: IV antibiotics. Urgent ERCP <24h. Biliary drainage. ICU/HDU monitoring.

pathways.criteria: Organ dysfunction (CV, resp, renal, hepatic, neurological), septic shock
pathways.treatment: ICU. IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (Meropenem). Vasopressors. Emergency ERCP <12h or PTC. Biliary drainage. Organ support.

operative_templates.procedure: ERCP with Sphincterotomy and Stone Extraction
steps: Patient positioned prone or left lateral under conscious sedation/GA
Pass duodenoscope to second part of duodenum
Identify ampulla of Vater — cannulate with sphincterotome
Confirm biliary access with contrast injection — cholangiogram
Perform sphincterotomy: cut in 11-12 o'clock direction using blended current
Extract stones using Dormia basket or extraction balloon
If complete clearance not possible: place plastic biliary stent (7-10 Fr) for drainage
Consider nasobiliary drain if severe cholangitis
Document stone clearance with completion cholangiogram

watchFor: stent occlusion if stent in place

dischargeCriteria: Afebrile for 48 hours without antibiotics (or step-down to oral)
Bilirubin and cholestatic enzymes trending down significantly
Plan for definitive management (cholecystectomy or stent exchange) confirmed

patient_education.explanation: Acute cholangitis is a severe infection of the bile ducts. It happens when a gallstone or other blockage traps bacteria in the bile ducts, causing the infection to spread into the bloodstream. This is a life-threatening emergency that requires urgent drainage of the bile ducts (usually with an ERCP) and strong antibiotics.
patient_education.warningSigns: Go to the nearest hospital immediately if: you develop fever with shaking chills, you turn yellow, you have right upper abdominal pain, you feel confused or drowsy, or you have dark urine and pale stools.
patient_education.activity: Rest during the acute phase. After recovery, you can resume normal activities gradually. If you had an ERCP, rest for 24-48 hours. Avoid heavy lifting for 1-2 weeks.
patient_education.diet: Start with clear fluids when pain-free, then advance to a soft diet. After recovery, eat a balanced diet low in fatty foods. Drink plenty of water. Avoid alcohol. For gallstone prevention: eat regular meals, include fiber (vegetables, whole grains), and maintain a healthy weight.
patient_education.medication: Complete the full course of antibiotics as prescribed. Do not stop early even if you feel better. Take pain medication as needed. If you have a biliary stent, you may need antibiotics before dental procedures (ask your doctor).
patient_education.followUp: Return in 2 weeks for clinical review. Complete LFTs in 1-2 weeks to confirm resolution. Schedule laparoscopic cholecystectomy within 4-6 weeks if stones were the cause. If you have a stent, plan for exchange or removal in 3-6 months.

after_discharge.action: Final review, surveillance imaging if indicated

when_to_return: Return of jaundice or dark urine
Vomiting or inability to keep fluids down
